  "content": "{\"content\":\"340:         # when running queries.\\n341:         with patch.object(connection, \\\"is_usable\\\", side_effect=AssertionError):\\n342:             self.run_query()\\n343: \\n344:     @skipUnlessDBFeature(\\\"test_db_allows_multiple_connections\\\")\\n345:     def test_health_checks_disabled(self):\\n346:         self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=False)\\n347:         self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\\n348:         # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing\\n349:         # the health check.\\n350:         with patch.object(connection, \\\"is_usable\\\", side_effect=AssertionError):\\n351:             self.run_query()\\n352: \\n353:         old_connection = connection.connection\\n354:         # Simulate request_finished.\\n355:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\\n356:         # Persistent connections are enabled (connection is not).\\n357:         self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\\n358:         # Health checks are not performed.\\n359:         with patch.object(connection, \\\"is_usable\\\", side_effect=AssertionError):\\n360:             self.run_query()\\n361:             # Health check wasn't performed and the connection is unchanged.\\n362:             self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\\n363:             self.run_query()\\n364:             # The connection is unchanged after the next query either during\\n365:             # the current \\\"request\\\".\\n366:             self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\\n367: \\n368:     @skipUnlessDBFeature(\\\"test_db_allows_multiple_connections\\\")\\n369:     def test_set_autocommit_health_checks_enabled(self):\\n370:         self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=True)\\n371:         self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\\n372:         # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing\\n373:         # the health check.\\n374:         with patch.object(connection, \\\"is_usable\\\", side_effect=AssertionError):\\n375:             # Simulate outermost atomic block: changing autocommit for\\n376:             # a connection.\\n377:             connection.set_autocommit(False)\\n378:             self.run_query()\\n379:             connection.commit()\\n380:             connection.set_autocommit(True)\\n381: \\n382:         old_connection = connection.connection\\n383:         # Simulate request_finished.\\n384:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\\n385:         # Persistent connections are enabled.\\n386:         self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\\n387: \\n388:         # Simulate connection health check failing.\\n389:         with patch.object(\\n390:             connection, \\\"is_usable\\\", return_value=False\\n391:         ) as mocked_is_usable:\\n392:             # Simulate outermost atomic block: changing autocommit for\\n393:             # a connection.\\n394:             connection.set_autocommit(False)\\n395:             new_connection = connection.connection\\n396:             self.assertIsNot(new_connection, old_connection)\\n397:             # Only one health check per \\\"request\\\" is performed, so a query will\\n398:             # carry on even if the health check fails. This query succeeds\\n399:             # because the real connection is healthy and only the health check\\n400:             # failure is mocked.\\n401:             self.run_query()\\n402:             connection.commit()\\n403:             connection.set_autocommit(True)\\n404:             # The connection is unchanged.\\n405:             self.assertIs(new_connection, connection.connection)\\n406:         self.assertEqual(mocked_is_usable.call_count, 1)\\n407: \\n408:         # Simulate request_finished.\\n409:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\\n410:         # The underlying connection is being reused further with health checks\\n411:         # succeeding.\\n412:         connection.set_autocommit(False)\\n413:         self.run_query()\\n414:         connection.commit()\\n415:         connection.set_autocommit(True)\\n416:         self.assertIs(new_connection, connection.connection)\\n417: \\n418: \\n419: class MultiDatabaseTests(TestCase):\\n420:     databases = {\\\"default\\\", \\\"other\\\"}\\n421: \\n422:     def test_multi_database_init_connection_state_called_once(self):\\n423:         for db in self.databases:\\n424:             with self.subTest(database=db):\\n425:                 with patch.object(connections[db], \\\"commit\\\", return_value=None):\\n426:                     with patch.object(\\n427:                         connections[db],\\n428:                         \\\"check_database_version_supported\\\",\\n429:                     ) as mocked_check_database_version_supported:\\n430:                         connections[db].init_connection_state()\\n431: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/tests/backends/base/test_base.py%3FL340-430\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "340:         # when running queries.\n341:         with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n342:             self.run_query()\n343: \n344:     @skipUnlessDBFeature(\"test_db_allows_multiple_connections\")\n345:     def test_health_checks_disabled(self):\n346:         self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=False)\n347:         self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\n348:         # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing\n349:         # the health check.\n350:         with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n351:             self.run_query()\n352: \n353:         old_connection = connection.connection\n354:         # Simulate request_finished.\n355:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\n356:         # Persistent connections are enabled (connection is not).\n357:         self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\n358:         # Health checks are not performed.\n359:         with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n360:             self.run_query()\n361:             # Health check wasn't performed and the connection is unchanged.\n362:             self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\n363:             self.run_query()\n364:             # The connection is unchanged after the next query either during\n365:             # the current \"request\".\n366:             self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\n367: \n368:     @skipUnlessDBFeature(\"test_db_allows_multiple_connections\")\n369:     def test_set_autocommit_health_checks_enabled(self):\n370:         self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=True)\n371:         self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\n372:         # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing\n373:         # the health check.\n374:         with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n375:             # Simulate outermost atomic block: changing autocommit for\n376:             # a connection.\n377:             connection.set_autocommit(False)\n378:             self.run_query()\n379:             connection.commit()\n380:             connection.set_autocommit(True)\n381: \n382:         old_connection = connection.connection\n383:         # Simulate request_finished.\n384:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\n385:         # Persistent connections are enabled.\n386:         self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection)\n387: \n388:         # Simulate connection health check failing.\n389:         with patch.object(\n390:             connection, \"is_usable\", return_value=False\n391:         ) as mocked_is_usable:\n392:             # Simulate outermost atomic block: changing autocommit for\n393:             # a connection.\n394:             connection.set_autocommit(False)\n395:             new_connection = connection.connection\n396:             self.assertIsNot(new_connection, old_connection)\n397:             # Only one health check per \"request\" is performed, so a query will\n398:             # carry on even if the health check fails. This query succeeds\n399:             # because the real connection is healthy and only the health check\n400:             # failure is mocked.\n401:             self.run_query()\n402:             connection.commit()\n403:             connection.set_autocommit(True)\n404:             # The connection is unchanged.\n405:             self.assertIs(new_connection, connection.connection)\n406:         self.assertEqual(mocked_is_usable.call_count, 1)\n407: \n408:         # Simulate request_finished.\n409:         connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\n410:         # The underlying connection is being reused further with health checks\n411:         # succeeding.\n412:         connection.set_autocommit(False)\n413:         self.run_query()\n414:         connection.commit()\n415:         connection.set_autocommit(True)\n416:         self.assertIs(new_connection, connection.connection)\n417: \n418: \n419: class MultiDatabaseTests(TestCase):\n420:     databases = {\"default\", \"other\"}\n421: \n422:     def test_multi_database_init_connection_state_called_once(self):\n423:         for db in self.databases:\n424:             with self.subTest(database=db):\n425:                 with patch.object(connections[db], \"commit\", return_value=None):\n426:                     with patch.object(\n427:                         connections[db],\n428:                         \"check_database_version_supported\",\n429:                     ) as mocked_check_database_version_supported:\n430:                         connections[db].init_connection_state()\n431: ",

django.core.exceptions import ImproperlyConfigured\\n from django.http import (\\n     HttpResponse,\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":1},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":8,\"startLine\":1}}],\"path\":\"django/views/generic/base.py\"},{\"added\":109,\"deleted\":6,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" * Bug fixers: anyone can contribute by opening a pull request and working on a\\n   solution for a ticket.\\n \\n-* Reviewers: anyone can review pull requests and suggest improvements.\\n+* Reviewers: anyone can :ref:`review pull requests <reviewing-patches>` and\\n+  suggest improvements.\\n \\n * Mergers: people with commit access who make the final decision to merge a\\n   change.\\n \\n-Our Trac system is intentionally open to the public, and anyone can help by\\n-working on tickets. Django is a community project, and we encourage\\n-:ref:`triage and collaboration by the community\\n-<how-can-i-help-with-triaging>`. This could be you!\\n+.. admonition:: When we say anyone, we mean you!\\n+\\n+    Our Trac system and GitHub repositories are intentionally open to the\\n+    public. Anyone can help by working on tickets, triaging tickets and\\n+    reviewing changes. Django is a community project, and we encourage\\n+    :ref:`triage and collaboration by the community\\n+    <how-can-i-help-with-triaging>`. We truly want you to help!\\n \\n For example, here's the typical lifecycle of a ticket:\\n \\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":19,\"startLine\":63},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":15,\"startLine\":63},\"section\":\"vote of the :ref:`Steering Council <steering-council>`.\"},{\"body\":\" * Please **don't** promote your own tickets to \\\"Ready for checkin\\\". You\\n   may mark other people's tickets that you've reviewed as \\\"Ready for\\n   checkin\\\", but you should get at minimum one other community member to\\n-  review a patch that you submit.\\n+  :ref:`review a patch <reviewing-patches>` that you submit.\\n \\n * Please **don't** reverse a decision without posting a message to the\\n   `Django Forum`_ to find consensus.\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":418},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":414},\"section\":\"the ticket database:\"},{\"body\":\" Now, report your results on the Trac ticket, and please include the regression\\n test as an attachment. When someone writes a fix for the bug, they'll already\\n have your test as a starting point.\\n+\\n+.. _reviewing-patches:\\n+\\n+Reviewing patches\\n+=================\\n+\\n+A great way to learn the Django codebase is to help review patches. It allows\\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\\n+reviewing patches.\\n+\\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \\\"Accepted\\\"\\n+state with the flag \\\"has patch\\\" set and the flags \\\"patch needs improvement\\\",\\n+\\\"needs tests\\\" and \\\"needs documentation\\\" unset. There is also a set of\\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\\n+\\n+Code review process\\n+-------------------\\n+\\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\\n+\\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_. If you're new, pick\\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\\n+\\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\\n+\\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\\n+\\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\\n+   correct?\\n+\\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\\n+\\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\\n+   into?\\n+\\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\\n... [diff truncated]\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":102,\"startLine\":488},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":3,\"startLine\":484},\"section\":\"commit where the test fails.\"}],\"path\":\"docs/internals/contributing/triaging-tickets.txt\"},{\"added\":2,\"deleted\":0,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\"      $ source .venv/bin/activate\\n      $ python -m pip install -r docs/requirements.txt\\n \\n+.. _build-documentation-locally:\\n+\\n Build the documentation locally\\n -------------------------------\\n \\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":8,\"startLine\":124},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":6,\"startLine\":124},\"section\":\"Create and activate a virtual environment, then install the dependencies:\"}],\"path\":\"docs/internals/contributing/writing-documentation.txt\"},{\"added\":6,\"deleted\":6,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\"     >>> blogs = Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\\\"entry\\\")).filter(entries__gt=5)\\n \\n ``alias()`` can be used in conjunction with :meth:`annotate`, :meth:`exclude`,\\n-:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use aliased expression\\n-with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to an\\n-annotation::\\n+:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use an aliased\\n+expression with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to\\n+an annotation::\\n \\n     Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\\\"entry\\\")).annotate(\\n         entries=F(\\\"entries\\\"),\\n     ).aggregate(Sum(\\\"entries\\\"))\\n \\n :meth:`filter` and :meth:`order_by` can take expressions directly, but\\n expression construction and usage often does not happen in the same place (for\\n example, ``QuerySet`` method creates expressions, for later use in views).\\n-``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally, possibly\\n-spanning multiple methods and modules, refer to the expression parts by their\\n-aliases and only use :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\\n+``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally (possibly\\n+spanning multiple methods and modules), referring to the expression parts by\\n+their aliases, and only using :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\\n \\n ``order_by()``\\n ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":20,\"startLine\":330},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":20,\"startLine\":330},\"section\":\"interested in the exact number of entries, you could do this:\"}],\"path\":\"docs/ref/models/querysets.txt\"},{\"added\":1,\"deleted\":0,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" needsnewfeatureprocess\\n n\u011bmecky\\n nginx\\n+nitpicky\\n noding\\n nonces\\n nonnegative\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":316},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":6,\"startLine\":316},\"section\":\"needsinfo\"}],\"path\":\"docs/spelling_wordlist\"},{\"added\":2,\"deleted\":2,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" \\n .. note::\\n \\n-    Django uses ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\\n+    Django uses ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\\n     asynchronous or not. If you implement your own method of returning a\\n-    coroutine, ensure you use ``asgiref.sync.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\\n+    coroutine, ensure you use ``inspect.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\\n     function returns ``True``.\\n \\n Under a WSGI server, async views will run in their own, one-off event loop.\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":9,\"startLine\":28},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":9,\"startLine\":28},\"section\":\"class-based view, this means declaring the HTTP method handlers, such as\"}],\"path\":\"docs/topics/async.txt\"},{\"added\":3,\"deleted\":3,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" ``async_capable = True``, then Django will pass it the request without\\n converting it. In this case, you can work out if your middleware will receive\\n async requests by checking if the ``get_response`` object you are passed is a\\n-coroutine function, using ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction``.\\n+coroutine function, using ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction``.\\n \\n The ``django.utils.decorators`` module contains\\n         "path": "django/views/generic/base.py"
      },
      {
        "added": 109,
        "deleted": 6,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " * Bug fixers: anyone can contribute by opening a pull request and working on a\n   solution for a ticket.\n \n-* Reviewers: anyone can review pull requests and suggest improvements.\n+* Reviewers: anyone can :ref:`review pull requests <reviewing-patches>` and\n+  suggest improvements.\n \n * Mergers: people with commit access who make the final decision to merge a\n   change.\n \n-Our Trac system is intentionally open to the public, and anyone can help by\n-working on tickets. Django is a community project, and we encourage\n-:ref:`triage and collaboration by the community\n-<how-can-i-help-with-triaging>`. This could be you!\n+.. admonition:: When we say anyone, we mean you!\n+\n+    Our Trac system and GitHub repositories are intentionally open to the\n+    public. Anyone can help by working on tickets, triaging tickets and\n+    reviewing changes. Django is a community project, and we encourage\n+    :ref:`triage and collaboration by the community\n+    <how-can-i-help-with-triaging>`. We truly want you to help!\n \n For example, here's the typical lifecycle of a ticket:\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 19,
              "startLine": 63
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 15,
              "startLine": 63
            },
            "section": "vote of the :ref:`Steering Council <steering-council>`."
          },
          {
            "body": " * Please **don't** promote your own tickets to \"Ready for checkin\". You\n   may mark other people's tickets that you've reviewed as \"Ready for\n   checkin\", but you should get at minimum one other community member to\n-  review a patch that you submit.\n+  :ref:`review a patch <reviewing-patches>` that you submit.\n \n * Please **don't** reverse a decision without posting a message to the\n   `Django Forum`_ to find consensus.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 418
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 414
            },
            "section": "the ticket database:"
          },
          {
            "body": " Now, report your results on the Trac ticket, and please include the regression\n test as an attachment. When someone writes a fix for the bug, they'll already\n have your test as a starting point.\n+\n+.. _reviewing-patches:\n+\n+Reviewing patches\n+=================\n+\n+A great way to learn the Django codebase is to help review patches. It allows\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\n+reviewing patches.\n+\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \"Accepted\"\n+state with the flag \"has patch\" set and the flags \"patch needs improvement\",\n+\"needs tests\" and \"needs documentation\" unset. There is also a set of\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\n+\n+Code review process\n+-------------------\n+\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\n+\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_. If you're new, pick\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\n+\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\n+\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\n+\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\n+   correct?\n+\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\n+\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\n+   into?\n+\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\n... [diff truncated]",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 102,
              "startLine": 488
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 3,
              "startLine": 484
            },
            "section": "commit where the test fails."
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/internals/contributing/triaging-tickets.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 2,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "      $ source .venv/bin/activate\n      $ python -m pip install -r docs/requirements.txt\n \n+.. _build-documentation-locally:\n+\n Build the documentation locally\n -------------------------------\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 8,
              "startLine": 124
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 6,
              "startLine": 124
            },
            "section": "Create and activate a virtual environment, then install the dependencies:"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/internals/contributing/writing-documentation.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 6,
        "deleted": 6,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "     >>> blogs = Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\"entry\")).filter(entries__gt=5)\n \n ``alias()`` can be used in conjunction with :meth:`annotate`, :meth:`exclude`,\n-:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use aliased expression\n-with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to an\n-annotation::\n+:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use an aliased\n+expression with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to\n+an annotation::\n \n     Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\"entry\")).annotate(\n         entries=F(\"entries\"),\n     ).aggregate(Sum(\"entries\"))\n \n :meth:`filter` and :meth:`order_by` can take expressions directly, but\n expression construction and usage often does not happen in the same place (for\n example, ``QuerySet`` method creates expressions, for later use in views).\n-``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally, possibly\n-spanning multiple methods and modules, refer to the expression parts by their\n-aliases and only use :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\n+``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally (possibly\n+spanning multiple methods and modules), referring to the expression parts by\n+their aliases, and only using :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\n \n ``order_by()``\n ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 20,
              "startLine": 330
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 20,
              "startLine": 330
            },
            "section": "interested in the exact number of entries, you could do this:"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/models/querysets.txt"
      },

        "added": 1,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [

      {
        "added": 2,
        "deleted": 2,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " \n .. note::\n \n-    Django uses ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\n+    Django uses ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\n     asynchronous or not. If you implement your own method of returning a\n-    coroutine, ensure you use ``asgiref.sync.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\n+    coroutine, ensure you use ``inspect.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\n     function returns ``True``.\n \n Under a WSGI server, async views will run in their own, one-off event loop.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 28
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 28
            },
            "section": "class-based view, this means declaring the HTTP method handlers, such as"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/topics/async.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 3,
        "deleted": 3,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " ``async_capable = True``, then Django will pass it the request without\n converting it. In this case, you can work out if your middleware will receive\n async requests by checking if the ``get_response`` object you are passed is a\n-coroutine function, using ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction``.\n+coroutine function, using ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction``.\n \n The ``django.utils.decorators`` module contains\n :func:`~django.utils.decorators.sync_only_middleware`,\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 318
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 318
            },
            "section": "If your middleware has both ``sync_capable = True`` and"
          },
          {
            "body": " \n Here's an example of how to create a middleware function that supports both::\n \n-    from asgiref.sync import iscoroutinefunction\n+    from inspect import iscoroutinefunction\n     from django.utils.decorators import sync_and_async_middleware\n \n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 337
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 337
            },
            "section": "at an additional performance penalty."
          },
          {
            "body": " When using an asynchronous class-based middleware, you must ensure that\n instances are correctly marked as coroutine functions::\n \n-    from asgiref.sync import iscoroutinefunction, markcoroutinefunction\n+    from inspect import iscoroutinefunction, markcoroutinefunction\n \n \n     class AsyncMiddleware:\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 373
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 373
            },
            "section": "Here's an example of how to create a middleware function that supports both::"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/topics/http/middleware.txt"
      },

Then from the\\n-``docs`` directory, run:\\n+You'll need to install :pypi:`sphinxcontrib-spelling` first. The spell checker\\n+also requires a system-level spell checking backend such as `Aspell\\n+<http://aspell.net/>`__. Then from the ``docs`` directory, run:\\n \\n .. console::\\n \\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":9,\"startLine\":178},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":8,\"startLine\":178},\"section\":\"Spelling check\"}],\"path\":\"docs/internals/contributing/writing-documentation.txt\"},{\"added\":7,\"deleted\":0,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" class ProxyUser(User):\\n     class Meta:\\n         proxy = True\\n+\\n+\\n+class MixedFieldsModel(models.Model):\\n+    \\\"\\\"\\\"Model with multiple field types for testing search validation.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+\\n+    int_field = models.IntegerField(null=True, blank=True)\\n+    json_field = models.JSONField(null=True, blank=True)\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":10,\"startLine\":140},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":3,\"startLine\":140},\"section\":\"class CharPK(models.Model):\"}],\"path\":\"tests/admin_changelist/models.py\"},{\"added\":84,\"deleted\":0,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\"     Group,\\n     Invitation,\\n     Membership,\\n+    MixedFieldsModel,\\n     Musician,\\n     OrderedObject,\\n     Parent,\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":66},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":6,\"startLine\":66},\"section\":\"from .models import (\"},{\"body\":\"         cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\\n         self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [abcd])\\n \\n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_invalid_value(self):\\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\\\"Test\\\", age=10)\\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\\n+        m.search_fields = [\\\"pk__exact\\\"]\\n+\\n+        request = self.factory.get(\\\"/\\\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \\\"foo\\\"})\\n+        request.user = self.superuser\\n+\\n+        # Invalid values are gracefully ignored.\\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\\n+\\n+    def test_exact_lookup_mixed_terms(self):\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        Multi-term search validates each term independently.\\n+\\n+        For 'foo 123' with search_fields=['name__icontains', 'age__exact']:\\n+        - 'foo': age lookup skipped (invalid), name lookup used\\n+        - '123': both lookups used (valid for age)\\n+        No Cast should be used; invalid lookups are simply skipped.\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        child = Child.objects.create(name=\\\"foo123\\\", age=123)\\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\\\"other\\\", age=456)\\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\\n+        m.search_fields = [\\\"name__icontains\\\", \\\"age__exact\\\"]\\n+\\n+        request = self.factory.get(\\\"/\\\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \\\"foo 123\\\"})\\n+        request.user = self.superuser\\n+\\n+        # One result matching on foo and 123.\\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [child])\\n+\\n+        # \\\"xyz\\\" - invalid for age (skipped), no match for name either.\\n+        request = self.factory.get(\\\"/\\\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \\\"xyz\\\"})\\n+        request.user = self.superuser\\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\\n+\\n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_more_lenient_formfield(self):\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        Exact lookups on BooleanField use formfield().to_python() for lenient\\n+        parsing. Using model field's to_python() would reject 'false' whereas\\n+        the form field accepts it.\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        obj = UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=False)\\n+        UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=True)\\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(UnorderedObject, custom_site)\\n+        m.search_fields = [\\\"bool__exact\\\"]\\n+\\n+        # 'false' is accepted by form field but rejected by model field.\\n+        request = self.factory.get(\\\"/\\\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \\\"false\\\"})\\n+        request.user = self.superuser\\n+\\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [obj])\\n+\\n+    def test_exact_lookup_validates_each_field_independently(self):\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        Each field validates the search term independently without leaking\\n+        converted values between fields.\\n+\\n+        \\\"3.\\\" is valid for IntegerField (converts to 3) but invalid for\\n+        JSONField. The converted value must not leak to the JSONField check.\\n+        \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n+        # obj_int has int_field=3, should match \\\"3.\\\" via IntegerField.\\n+        obj_int = MixedFieldsModel.objects.create(\\n+    \\n... [diff truncated]\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":89,\"startLine\":857},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":6,\"startLine\":856},\"section\":\"class ChangeListTests(TestCase):\"}],\"path\":\"tests/admin_changelist/tests.py\"},{\"added\":4,\"deleted\":0,\"hunks\":[{\"body\":\" class CamelCaseRelatedModel(models.Model):\\n     m2m = models.ManyToManyField(CamelCaseModel, related_name=\\\"m2m\\\")\\n     fk = models.ForeignKey(CamelCaseModel, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name=\\\"fk\\\")\\n+    # Add another relation that will not participate in filter_horizontal.\\n+    fk2 = models.ForeignKey(\\n+        CamelCaseModel, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name=\\\"fk2\\\"\\n+    )\\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":7,\"startLine\":1196},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":3,\"startLine\":1196},\"section\":\"class CamelCaseModel(models.Model):\"}],\"path\":\"tests/admin_views/models.py\"}],\"hasNextPage\":false,\"head\":\"HEAD~5\",\"repository\":\"github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c\",\"totalAdded\":143,\"totalDeleted\":29,\"totalModified\":7}",

            "body": " ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n \n Before you commit your docs, it's a good idea to run the spelling checker.\n-You'll need to install :pypi:`sphinxcontrib-spelling` first. Then from the\n-``docs`` directory, run:\n+You'll need to install :pypi:`sphinxcontrib-spelling` first.             "body": " class ProxyUser(User):\n     class Meta:\n         proxy = True\n+\n+\n+class MixedFieldsModel(models.Model):\n+    \"\"\"Model with multiple field types for testing search validation.\"\"\"\n+\n+    int_field = models.IntegerField(null=True, blank=True)\n+    json_field = models.JSONField(null=True, blank=True)\n",

      },
      {
        "added": 84,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "     Group,\n     Invitation,\n     Membership,\n+    MixedFieldsModel,\n     Musician,\n     OrderedObject,\n     Parent,\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,

            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 6,

            "section": "from .models import ("
          },
          {
            "body": "         cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n         self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [abcd])\n \n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_invalid_value(self):\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\"Test\", age=10)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"pk__exact\"]\n+\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"foo\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        # Invalid values are gracefully ignored.\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\n+\n+    def test_exact_lookup_mixed_terms(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Multi-term search validates each term independently.\n+\n+        For 'foo 123' with search_fields=['name__icontains', 'age__exact']:\n+        - 'foo': age lookup skipped (invalid), name lookup used\n+        - '123': both lookups used (valid for age)\n+        No Cast should be used; invalid lookups are simply skipped.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        child = Child.objects.create(name=\"foo123\", age=123)\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\"other\", age=456)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"name__icontains\", \"age__exact\"]\n+\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"foo 123\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        # One result matching on foo and 123.\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [child])\n+\n+        # \"xyz\" - invalid for age (skipped), no match for name either.\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"xyz\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\n+\n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_more_lenient_formfield(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Exact lookups on BooleanField use formfield().to_python() for lenient\n+        parsing. Using model field's to_python() would reject 'false' whereas\n+        the form field accepts it.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        obj = UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=False)\n+        UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=True)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(UnorderedObject, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"bool__exact\"]\n+\n+        # 'false' is accepted by form field but rejected by model field.\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"false\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [obj])\n+\n+    def test_exact_lookup_validates_each_field_independently(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Each field validates the search term independently without leaking\n+        converted values between fields.\n+\n+        \"3.\" is valid for IntegerField (converts to 3) but invalid for\n+        JSONField. The converted value must not leak to the JSONField check.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        # obj_int has int_field=3, should match \"3.\" via IntegerField.\n+        obj_int = MixedFieldsModel.objects.create(\n+    \n... [diff truncated]",

  "content": "{\"content\":\"591:     def close_if_unusable_or_obsolete(self):\\n592:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n593:         Close the current connection if unrecoverable errors have occurred\\n594:         or if it outlived its maximum age.\\n595:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n596:         if self.connection is not None:\\n597:             self.health_check_done = False\\n598:             # If the application didn't restore the original autocommit\\n599:             # setting, don't take chances, drop the connection.\\n600:             if self.get_autocommit() != self.settings_dict[\\\"AUTOCOMMIT\\\"]:\\n601:                 self.close()\\n602:                 return\\n603: \\n604:             # If an exception other than DataError or IntegrityError occurred\\n605:             # since the last commit / rollback, check if the connection works.\\n606:             if self.errors_occurred:\\n607:                 if self.is_usable():\\n608:                     self.errors_occurred = False\\n609:                     self.health_check_done = True\\n610:                 else:\\n611:                     self.close()\\n612:                     return\\n613: \\n614:             if self.close_at is not None and time.monotonic() >= self.close_at:\\n615:                 self.close()\\n616:                 return\\n617: \\n618:     # ##### Thread safety handling #####\\n619: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/base/base.py%3FL591-618\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "591:     def close_if_unusable_or_obsolete(self):\n592:         \"\"\"\n593:         Close the current connection if unrecoverable errors have occurred\n594:         or if it outlived its maximum age.\n595:         \"\"\"\n596:         if self.connection is not None:\n597:             self.health_check_done = False\n598:             # If the application didn't restore the original autocommit\n599:             # setting, don't take chances, drop the connection.\n600:             if self.get_autocommit() != self.settings_dict[\"AUTOCOMMIT\"]:\n601:                 self.close()\n602:                 return\n603: \n604:             # If an exception other than DataError or IntegrityError occurred\n605:             # since the last commit / rollback, check if the connection works.\n606:             if self.errors_occurred:\n607:                 if self.is_usable():\n608:                     self.errors_occurred = False\n609:                     self.health_check_done = True\n610:                 else:\n611:                     self.close()\n612:                     return\n613: \n614:             if self.close_at is not None and time.monotonic() >= self.close_at:\n615:                 self.close()\n616:                 return\n617: \n618:     # ##### Thread safety handling #####\n619: ",
    "url": "https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/base/base.py%3FL591-618"
  }
}

  "content": "{\"content\":\"236:     @async_unsafe\\n237:     def connect(self):\\n238:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Connect to the database. Assume that the connection is closed.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n239:         # Check for invalid configurations.\\n240:         self.check_settings()\\n241:         # In case the previous connection was closed while in an atomic block\\n242:         self.in_atomic_block = False\\n243:         self.savepoint_ids = []\\n244:         self.atomic_blocks = []\\n245:         self.needs_rollback = False\\n246:         # Reset parameters defining when to close/health-check the connection.\\n247:         self.health_check_enabled = self.settings_dict[\\\"CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS\\\"]\\n248:         max_age = self.settings_dict[\\\"CONN_MAX_AGE\\\"]\\n249:         self.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age\\n250:         self.closed_in_transaction = False\\n251:         self.errors_occurred = False\\n252:         # New connections are healthy.\\n253: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/base/base.py%3FL236-252\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "236:     @async_unsafe\n237:     def connect(self):\n238:         \"\"\"Connect to the database. Assume that the connection is closed.\"\"\"\n239:         # Check for invalid configurations.\n240:         self.check_settings()\n241:         # In case the previous connection was closed while in an atomic block\n242:         self.in_atomic_block = False\n243:         self.savepoint_ids = []\n244:         self.atomic_blocks = []\n245:         self.needs_rollback = False\n246:         # Reset parameters defining when to close/health-check the connection.\n247:         self.health_check_enabled = self.settings_dict[\"CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS\"]\n248:         max_age = self.settings_dict[\"CONN_MAX_AGE\"]\n249:         self.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age\n250:         self.closed_in_transaction = False\n251:         self.errors_occurred = False\n252:         # New connections are healthy.\n253: ",

Do nothing if savepoints are not supported.\\n408:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n409:         if not self._savepoint_allowed():\\n410:             return\\n411: \\n412:         self.validate_thread_sharing()\\n413:         self._savepoint_rollback(sid)\\n414: \\n415:         # Remove any callbacks registered while this savepoint was active.\\n416:         self.run_on_commit = [\\n417:             (sids, func, robust)\\n418:             for (sids, func, robust) in self.run_on_commit\\n419:             if sid not in sids\\n420:         ]\\n421: \\n422:     @async_unsafe\\n423:     def savepoint_commit(self, sid):\\n424:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n425:         Release a savepoint. Do nothing if savepoints are not supported.\\n426:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n427:         if not self._savepoint_allowed():\\n428:             return\\n429: \\n430:         self.validate_thread_sharing()\\n431:         self._savepoint_commit(sid)\\n432: \\n433:     @async_unsafe\\n434:     def clean_savepoints(self):\\n435:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n436:         Reset the counter used to generate unique savepoint ids in this thread.\\n437:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n438:         self.savepoint_state = 0\\n439: \\n440:     # ##### Backend-specific transaction management methods #####\\n441: \\n442:     def _set_autocommit(self, autocommit):\\n443:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n444:         Backend-specific implementation to enable or disable autocommit.\\n445:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n446:         raise NotImplementedError(\\n447:             \\\"subclasses of BaseDatabaseWrapper may require a _set_autocommit() method\\\"\\n448:         )\\n449: \\n450:     # ##### Generic transaction management methods #####\\n451: \\n452:     def get_autocommit(self):\\n453:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Get the autocommit state.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n454:         self.ensure_connection()\\n455:         return self.autocommit\\n456: \\n457:     def set_autocommit(\\n458:         self, autocommit, force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit=False\\n459:     ):\\n460:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n461:         Enable or disable autocommit.\\n462: \\n463:         The usual way to start a transaction is to turn autocommit off.\\n464:         SQLite does not properly start a transaction when disabling\\n465:         autocommit. To avoid this buggy behavior and to actually enter a new\\n466:         transaction, an explicit BEGIN is required. Using\\n467:         force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit=True will issue an\\n468:         explicit BEGIN with SQLite. This option will be ignored for other\\n469:         backends.\\n470:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n471:         self.validate_no_atomic_block()\\n472:         self.close_if_health_check_failed()\\n473:         self.ensure_connection()\\n474: \\n475:         start_transaction_under_autocommit = (\\n476:             force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit\\n477:             and not autocommit\\n478:             and hasattr(self, \\\"_start_transaction_under_autocommit\\\")\\n479:         )\\n480: \\n481:         if start_transaction_under_autocommit:\\n482:             self._start_transaction_under_autocommit()\\n483:         elif autocommit:\\n484:             self._set_autocommit(autocommit)\\n485:         else:\\n486:             with debug_transaction(self, \\\"BEGIN\\\"):\\n487:                 self._set_autocommit(autocommit)\\n488:         self.autocommit = autocommit\\n489: \\n490:         if autocommit and self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on:\\n491:             self.run_and_clear_commit_hooks()\\n492:             self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on = False\\n493: \\n494:     def get_rollback(self):\\n495:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Get the \\\"needs rollback\\\" flag -- for *advanced use* only.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n496:         if not self.in_atomic_block:\\n497:             raise TransactionManagementError(\\n498:                 \\\"The rollback flag doesn't work outside of an 'atomic' block.\\\"\\n499:             )\\n500:         return self.needs_rollback\\n501: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/base/base.py%3FL400-500\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "400:         self._savepoint(sid)\n401: \n402:         return sid\n403: \n404:     @async_unsafe\n405:     def savepoint_rollback(self, sid):\n406:         \"\"\"\n407:         Roll back to a savepoint. Do nothing if savepoints are not supported.\n408:         \"\"\"\n409:         if not self._savepoint_allowed():\n410:             return\n411: \n412:         self.validate_thread_sharing()\n413:         self._savepoint_rollback(sid)\n414: \n415:         # Remove any callbacks registered while this savepoint was active.\n416:         self.run_on_commit = [\n417:             (sids, func, robust)\n418:             for (sids, func, robust) in self.run_on_commit\n419:             if sid not in sids\n420:         ]\n421: \n422:     @async_unsafe\n423:     def savepoint_commit(self, sid):\n424:         \"\"\"\n425:         Release a savepoint. To avoid this buggy behavior and to actually enter a new\n466:         transaction, an explicit BEGIN is required. Using\n467:         force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit=True will issue an\n468:         explicit BEGIN with SQLite. This option will be ignored for other\n469:         backends.\n470:         \"\"\"\n471:         self.validate_no_atomic_block()\n472:         self.close_if_health_check_failed()\n473:         self.ensure_connection()\n474: \n475:         start_transaction_under_autocommit = (\n476:             force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit\n477:             and not autocommit\n478:             and hasattr(self, \"_start_transaction_under_autocommit\")\n479:         )\n480: \n481:         if start_transaction_under_autocommit:\n482:             self._start_transaction_under_autocommit()\n483:         elif autocommit:\n484:             self._set_autocommit(autocommit)\n485:         else:\n486:             with debug_transaction(self, \"BEGIN\"):\n487:                 self._set_autocommit(autocommit)\n488:         self.autocommit = autocommit\n489: \n490:         if autocommit and self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on:\n491:             self.run_and_clear_commit_hooks()\n492:             self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on = False\n493: \n494:     def get_rollback(self):\n495:         \"\"\"Get the \"needs rollback\" flag -- for *advanced use* only.\"\"\"\n496:         if not self.in_atomic_block:\n497:             raise TransactionManagementError(\n498:                 \"The rollback flag doesn't work outside of an 'atomic' block.\"\n499:             )\n500:         return self.needs_rollback\n501: ",

##### Backend-specific wrappers for PEP-249 connection methods #####\\n282: \\n283:     def _prepare_cursor(self, cursor):\\n284:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n285:         Validate the connection is usable and perform database cursor wrapping.\\n286:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n287:         self.validate_thread_sharing()\\n288:         if self.queries_logged:\\n289:             wrapped_cursor = self.make_debug_cursor(cursor)\\n290:         else:\\n291:             wrapped_cursor = self.make_cursor(cursor)\\n292:         return wrapped_cursor\\n293: \\n294:     def _cursor(self, name=None):\\n295:         self.close_if_health_check_failed()\\n296:         self.ensure_connection()\\n297:         with self.wrap_database_errors:\\n298:             return self._prepare_cursor(self.create_cursor(name))\\n299: \\n300:     def _commit(self):\\n301:         if self.connection is not None:\\n302:             with debug_transaction(self, \\\"COMMIT\\\"), self.wrap_database_errors:\\n303:                 return self.connection.commit()\\n304: \\n305:     def _rollback(self):\\n306:         if self.connection is not None:\\n307:             with debug_transaction(self, \\\"ROLLBACK\\\"), self.wrap_database_errors:\\n308:                 return self.connection.rollback()\\n309: \\n310:     def _close(self):\\n311:         if self.connection is not None:\\n312:             with self.wrap_database_errors:\\n313:                 return self.connection.close()\\n314: \\n315:     # The next connect()\\n351:         # will reset the transaction state anyway.\\n352:         if self.closed_in_transaction or self.connection is None:\\n353:             return\\n354:         try:\\n355:             self._close()\\n356:         finally:\\n357:             if self.in_atomic_block:\\n358:                 self.closed_in_transaction = True\\n359:                 self.needs_rollback = True\\n360:             else:\\n361:                 self.connection = None\\n362: \\n363:     # ##### Backend-specific savepoint management methods #####\\n364: \\n365:     def _savepoint(self, sid):\\n366:         with self.cursor() as cursor:\\n367:             cursor.execute(self.ops.savepoint_create_sql(sid))\\n368: \\n369:     def _savepoint_rollback(self, sid):\\n370:         with self.cursor() as cursor:\\n371:             cursor.execute(self.ops.savepoint_rollback_sql(sid))\\n372: \\n373:     def _savepoint_commit(self, sid):\\n374:         with self.cursor() as cursor:\\n375:             cursor.execute(self.ops.savepoint_commit_sql(sid))\\n376: \\n377:     def _savepoint_allowed(self):\\n378:         # Savepoints cannot be created outside a transaction\\n379:         return self.features.uses_savepoints and not self.get_autocommit()\\n380: \\n381:     # ##### Generic savepoint management methods #####\\n382: \\n383:     @async_unsafe\\n384:     def savepoint(self):\\n385:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n386:         Create a savepoint inside the current transaction.             "content": "270: \n271: Django includes default views and templates for several HTTP error codes.           {
            "content": "37: \n38: The default value is ``0``, preserving the historical behavior of closing the\n39: database connection at the end of each request.             "endLine": 42,
            "startLine": 37
          },
          {
            "content": "551: ``\"AA\"`` into the same column, since they compare as equal (and, hence,\n552: non-unique) with the default collation. If you want case-sensitive comparisons\n553: on a particular column or table, change the column or table to use the\n554: ``utf8mb4_0900_as_cs`` collation.\n555: \n556: ",
            "endLine": 556,
            "startLine": 551
          },
          {
            "content": "545: \n546: By default, with a UTF-8 database, MySQL will use the\n547: ``utf8mb4_0900_ai_ci`` collation. This results in all string equality\n548: comparisons being done in a *case-insensitive* manner. That is, ``\"Fred\"`` and\n549: ",
            "endLine": 549,

\",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/pyproject.toml\"}",

    "content": "1: [build-system]\n2: requires = [\"setuptools>=77.0.3\"]\n3: build-backend = \"setuptools.build_meta\"\n4: \n5: [project]\n6: name = \"Django\"\n7: dynamic = [\"version\"]\n8: requires-python = \">= 3.12\"\n9: dependencies = [\n10:     \"asgiref>=3.9.1\",\n11:     \"sqlparse>=0.5.0\",\n12:     \"tzdata; sys_platform == 'win32'\",\n13: ]\n14: authors = [\n15:   {name = \"Django Software Foundation\", email = \"foundation@djangoproject.com\"},\n16: ]\n17: description = \"A high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design.\"\n18: readme = \"README.rst\"\n19: license = \"BSD-3-Clause\"\n20: license-files = [\"LICENSE\", \"LICENSE.python\"]\n21: classifiers = [\n22:     \"Development Status :: 2 - Pre-Alpha\",\n23:     \"Environment :: Web Environment\",\n24:     \"Framework :: Django\",\n25:     \"Intended Audience :: Developers\",\n26:     \"Operating System :: OS Independent\",\n27:     \"Programming Language :: Python\",\n28:     \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3\",\n29:     \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3 :: Only\",\n30:     \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12\",\n31:     \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13\",\n32:     \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14\",\n33:     \"Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP\",\n34:     \"Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: Dynamic Content\",\n35:     \"Topic :: Internet :: WWW/HTTP :: WSGI\",\n36:     \"Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Application Frameworks\",\n37:     \"Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules\",\n38: ]\n39: \n40: [project.optional-dependencies]\n41: argon2 = [\"argon2-cffi>=23.1.0\"]\n42: bcrypt = [\"bcrypt>=4.1.1\"]\n43: \n44: [project.scripts]\n45: django-admin = \"django.core.management:execute_from_command_line\"\n46: \n47: [project.urls]\n48: Homepage = \"https://www.djangoproject.com/\"\n49: Documentation = \"https://docs.djangoproject.com/\"\n50: \"Release notes\" = \"https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/releases/\"\n51: Funding = \"https://www.djangoproject.com/fundraising/\"\n52: Source = \"https://github.com/django/django\"\n53: Tracker = \"https://code.djangoproject.com/\"\n54: \n55: [tool.black]\n56: target-version = [\"py312\"]\n57: force-exclude = \"tests/test_runner_apps/tagged/tests_syntax_error.py\"\n58: \n59: [tool.isort]\n60: profile = \"black\"\n61: default_section = \"THIRDPARTY\"\n62: known_first_party = \"django\"\n63: \n64: [tool.setuptools.dynamic]\n65: version = {attr = \"django.__version__\"}\n66: \n67: [tool.setuptools.packages.find]\n68: include = [\"django*\"]\n69: ",

  "content": "{\"content\":\"560:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n561:         pass\\n562: \\n563:     # ##### Connection termination handling #####\\n564: \\n565:     def is_usable(self):\\n566:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n567:         Test if the database connection is usable.\\n568: \\n569:         This method may assume that self.connection is not None.\\n570: \\n571:         Actual implementations should take care not to raise exceptions\\n572:         as that may prevent Django from recycling unusable connections.\\n573:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n574:         raise NotImplementedError(\\n575:             \\\"subclasses of BaseDatabaseWrapper may require an is_usable() method\\\"\\n576:         )\\n577: \\n578:     def close_if_health_check_failed(self):\\n579:         \\\"\\\"\\\"Close existing connection if it fails a health check.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n580:         if (\\n581:             self.connection is None\\n582:             or not self.health_check_enabled\\n583:             or self.health_check_done\\n584:         ):\\n585:             return\\n586: \\n587:         if not self.is_usable():\\n588:             self.close()\\n589:         self.health_check_done = True\\n590: \\n591:     def close_if_unusable_or_obsolete(self):\\n592:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n593:         Close the current connection if unrecoverable errors have occurred\\n594:         or if it outlived its maximum age.\\n595:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n596: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/base/base.py%3FL560-595\"}",

74. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "sed -n '590,620p' /home/claude/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/django/db/backends/base/base.py",
  "description": "Read close_if_unusable_or_obsolete in installed Django"
}
Output
{
  "interrupted": false,
  "isImage": false,
  "noOutputExpected": false,
  "stderr": "",
  "stdout": "    def close_if_unusable_or_obsolete(self):\n        \"\"\"\n        Close the current connection if unrecoverable errors have occurred\n        or if it outlived its maximum age.\n        \"\"\"\n        if self.connection is not None:\n            self.health_check_done = False\n            # If the application didn't restore the original autocommit setting,\n            # don't take chances, drop the connection.\n            if self.get_autocommit() != self.settings_dict[\"AUTOCOMMIT\"]:\n                self.close()\n                return\n\n            # If an exception other than DataError or IntegrityError occurred\n            # since the last commit / rollback, check if the connection works.\n            if self.errors_occurred:\n                if self.is_usable():\n                    self.errors_occurred = False\n                    self.health_check_done = True\n                else:\n                    self.close()\n                    return\n\n            if self.close_at is not None and time.monotonic() >= self.close_at:\n                self.close()\n                return\n\n    # ##### Thread safety handling #####\n\n    @property"
}
75. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "sed -n '236,260p' /home/claude/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/django/db/backends/base/base.py",
  "description": "Read connect() in installed Django"
}
Output
{
  "interrupted": false,
  "isImage": false,
  "noOutputExpected": false,
  "stderr": "",
  "stdout": "    @async_unsafe\n    def connect(self):\n        \"\"\"Connect to the database. Assume that the connection is closed.\"\"\"\n        # Check for invalid configurations.\n        self.check_settings()\n        # In case the previous connection was closed while in an atomic block\n        self.in_atomic_block = False\n        self.savepoint_ids = []\n        self.atomic_blocks = []\n        self.needs_rollback = False\n        # Reset parameters defining when to close/health-check the connection.\n        self.health_check_enabled = self.settings_dict[\"CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS\"]\n        max_age = self.settings_dict[\"CONN_MAX_AGE\"]\n        self.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age\n        self.closed_in_transaction = False\n        self.errors_occurred = False\n        # New connections are healthy.\n        self.health_check_done = True\n        # Establish the connection\n        conn_params = self.get_connection_params()\n        self.connection = self.get_new_connection(conn_params)\n        self.set_autocommit(self.settings_dict[\"AUTOCOMMIT\"])\n        self.init_connection_state()\n        connection_created.send(sender=self.__class__, connection=self)"
}

Output
{
  "content": "{\"content\":\"100:         # the active savepoint IDs when this function was registered and robust\\n101:         # specifies whether it's allowed for the function to fail.\\n102:         self.run_on_commit = []\\n103: \\n104:         # Should we run the on-commit hooks the next time set_autocommit(True)\\n105:         # is called?\\n106:         self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on = False\\n107: \\n108:         # A stack of wrappers to be invoked around execute()/executemany()\\n109:         # calls. Each entry is a function taking five arguments: execute, sql,\\n110:         # params, many, and context. It's the function's responsibility to\\n111:         # call execute(sql, params, many, context).\\n112:         self.execute_wrappers = []\\n113: \\n114:         self.client = self.client_class(self)\\n115:         self.creation = self.creation_class(self)\\n116:         self.features = self.features_class(self)\\n117:         self.introspection = self.introspection_class(self)\\n118:         self.ops = self.ops_class(self)\\n119:         self.validation = self.validation_class(self)\\n120: \\n121:     def __repr__(self):\\n122:         return (\\n123:             f\\\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__} \\\"\\n124:             f\\\"vendor={self.vendor!r} alias={self.alias!r}>\\\"\\n125:         )\\n126: \\n127:     def ensure_timezone(self):\\n128:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n129:         Ensure the connection's timezone is set to `self.timezone_name` and\\n130:         return whether it changed or not.\\n131:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n132:         return False\\n133: \\n134:     @cached_property\\n135:     def timezone(self):\\n136:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n137:         Return a tzinfo of the database connection time zone.\\n138: \\n139:         This is only used when time zone support is enabled. When a datetime is\\n140:         read from the database, it is always returned in this time zone.\\n141: \\n142:         When the database backend supports time zones, it doesn't matter which\\n143:         time zone Django uses, as long as aware datetimes are used everywhere.\\n144:         Other users connecting to the database can choose their own time zone.\\n145: \\n146:         When the database backend doesn't support time zones, the time zone\\n147:         Django uses may be constrained by the requirements of other users of\\n148:         the database.\\n149:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n150:         if not settings.USE_TZ:\\n151:             return None\\n152:         elif self.settings_dict[\\\"TIME_ZONE\\\"] is None:\\n153:             return datetime.UTC\\n154:         else:\\n155:             return zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(self.settings_dict[\\\"TIME_ZONE\\\"])\\n156: \\n157:     @cached_property\\n158:     def timezone_name(self):\\n159:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n160:         Name of the time zone of the database connection.\\n161:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n162:         if not settings.USE_TZ:\\n163:             return settings.TIME_ZONE\\n164:         elif self.settings_dict[\\\"TIME_ZONE\\\"] is None:\\n165:             return \\\"UTC\\\"\\n166:         else:\\n167:             return self.settings_dict[\\\"TIME_ZONE\\\"]\\n168: \\n169:     @property\\n170:     def queries_logged(self):\\n171:         return self.force_debug_cursor or settings.DEBUG\\n172: \\n173:     @property\\n174:     def queries(self):\\n175:         if len(self.queries_log) == self.queries_log.maxlen:\\n176:             warnings.warn(\\n177:                 \\\"Limit for query logging exceeded, only the last {} queries \\\"\\n178:   "structuredContent": {
    "content": "100:         # the active savepoint IDs when this function was registered and robust\n101:         # specifies whether it's allowed for the function to fail.\n102:         self.run_on_commit = []\n103: \n104:         # Should we run the on-commit hooks the next time set_autocommit(True)\n105:         # is called?\n106:         self.run_commit_hooks_on_set_autocommit_on = False\n107: \n108:         # A stack of wrappers to be invoked around execute()/executemany()\n109:         # calls. Each entry is a function taking five arguments: execute, sql,\n110:         # params, many, and context. It's the function's responsibility to\n111:         # call execute(sql, params, many, context).\n112:         self.execute_wrappers = []\n113: \n114:         self.client = self.client_class(self)\n115:         self.creation = self.creation_class(self)\n116:         self.features = self.features_class(self)\n117:         self.introspection = self.introspection_class(self)\n118:         self.ops = self.ops_class(self)\n119:         self.validation = self.validation_class(self)\n120: \n121:     def __repr__(self):\n122:         return (\n123:             f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__} \"\n124:             f\"vendor={self.vendor!r} alias={self.alias!r}>\"\n125:         )\n126: \n127:     def ensure_timezone(self):\n128:         \"\"\"\n129:         Ensure the connection's timezone is set to `self.timezone_name` and\n130:         return whether it changed or not.\n131:         \"\"\"\n132:         return False\n133: \n134:     @cached_property\n135:     def timezone(self):\n136:         \"\"\"\n137:         Return a tzinfo of the database connection time zone.\n138: \n139:         This is only used when time zone support is enabled. When a datetime is\n140:         read from the database, it is always returned in this time zone.\n141: \n142:         When the database backend supports time zones, it doesn't matter which\n143:         time zone Django uses, as long as aware datetimes are used everywhere.\n144:         Other users connecting to the database can choose their own time zone.\n145: \n146:         When the database backend doesn't support time zones, the time zone\n147:         Django uses may be constrained by the requirements of other users of\n148:         the database.\n149:         \"\"\"\n150:         if not settings.USE_TZ:\n151:             return None\n152:         elif self.settings_dict[\"TIME_ZONE\"] is None:\n153:             return datetime.UTC\n154:         else:\n155:             return zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(self.settings_dict[\"TIME_ZONE\"])\n156: \n157:     @cached_property\n158:     def timezone_name(self):\n159:         \"\"\"\n160:         Name of the time zone of the database connection.\n161:         \"\"\"\n162:         if not settings.USE_TZ:\n163:             return settings.TIME_ZONE\n164:         elif self.settings_dict[\"TIME_ZONE\"] is None:\n165:             return \"UTC\"\n166:         else:\n167:             return self.settings_dict[\"TIME_ZONE\"]\n168: \n169:     @property\n170:     def queries_logged(self):\n171:         return self.force_debug_cursor or settings.DEBUG\n172: \n173:     @property\n174:     def queries(self):\n175:         if len(self.queries_log) == self.queries_log.maxlen:\n176:             warnings.warn(\n177:                 \"Limit for query logging exceeded, only the last {} queries \"\n178:                 \"will be returned.\".format(self.queries_log.maxlen),\n179:                 stacklevel=2,\n180:             )\n181:         return list(self.queries_log)\n182: \n183:     def get_database_version(self):\n184:         \"\"\"Return a tuple of the database's version.\"\"\"\n185:         raise NotImplementedError(\n186:             \"subclasses of BaseDatabaseWrapper may require a get_database_version() \"\n187:             \"method.\"\n188:         )\n189: \n190:     def check_database_version_supported(self):\n191:         \"\"\"\n192:         Raise an error if the database version isn't supported by this\n193:         version of Django.\n194:         \"\"\"\n195:         if (\n196:             self.features.minimum_database_version is not None\n197:             and self.get_database_version() < self.features.minimum_database_version\n198:         ):\n199:             db_version = \".\".join(map(str, self.get_database_version()))\n200:             min_db_version = \".\".join(map(str, self.features.minimum_database_version))\n201: ",

80. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "python3.11 -c \"\nimport django\nfrom unittest.mock import patch\nfrom django.test import SimpleTestCase\n\n# Test if a connection with CONN_MAX_AGE=None keeps its close_at as None\nfrom django.db.backends.base.base import BaseDatabaseWrapper\nimport time\n\nclass MockWrapper:\n    settings_dict = {'CONN_MAX_AGE': None, 'CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS': False, 'AUTOCOMMIT': True, 'TIME_ZONE': None}\n    connection = None\n    autocommit = True\n    \nwrapper = BaseDatabaseWrapper.__new__(BaseDatabaseWrapper)\nwrapper.settings_dict = {'CONN_MAX_AGE': None, 'CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS': False, 'AUTOCOMMIT': True, 'TIME_ZONE': None}\nwrapper.close_at = None\n\n# Simulate what connect() does\nmax_age = wrapper.settings_dict['CONN_MAX_AGE']\nwrapper.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age\n\nprint('CONN_MAX_AGE = None, close_at =', wrapper.close_at)  # Should be None\n\n# Try with 0\nwrapper.settings_dict['CONN_MAX_AGE'] = 0\nmax_age = wrapper.settings_dict['CONN_MAX_AGE']\nwrapper.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age\nprint('CONN_MAX_AGE = 0, close_at =', wrapper.close_at)  # Should be a float (current time)\n\" 2>&1",
  "description": "Test the close_at logic directly"
}
Output
{
  "interrupted": false,
  "isImage": false,
  "noOutputExpected": false,
  "stderr": "",
  "stdout": "CONN_MAX_AGE = None, close_at = None\nCONN_MAX_AGE = 0, close_at = 20244872.606604975"
}

82. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_deepsearch @ -
Input
{
  "question": "In repo github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c, what is the bug in the database connection lifecycle management that causes persistent connections (CONN_MAX_AGE=None) to be closed prematurely? I've read close_if_unusable_or_obsolete in django/db/backends/base/base.py (line 591-616) and the connect() method (line 236-259) and they look correct. Is there another place in django/db/ that might close connections incorrectly when CONN_MAX_AGE=None? Look specifically for any incorrect condition that evaluates the connection age or max_age value."
}
Output
{
  "content": "{\"link\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/deepsearch/0471bb67-d3be-48e3-823c-86c3154a230c\",\"note\":\"Poll for results using sg_deepsearch_read with the link value. Inform the user that they can follow the deepsearch progress at the provided link\"}",

}

PostgreSQL backend-specific attributes.\\n190:     _named_cursor_idx = 0\\n191:     _connection_pools = {}\\n192: \\n193:     @property\\n194:     def pool(self):\\n195:         pool_options = self.settings_dict[\\\"OPTIONS\\\"].get(\\\"pool\\\")\\n196:         if self.alias == NO_DB_ALIAS or not pool_options:\\n197:             return None\\n198: \\n199:         if self.alias not in self._connection_pools:\\n200:             if self.settings_dict.get(\\\"CONN_MAX_AGE\\\", 0) != 0:\\n201:                 raise ImproperlyConfigured(\\n202:                     \\\"Pooling doesn't support persistent connections.\\\"\\n203:                 )\\n204:             # Set the default options.\\n205:             if pool_options is True:\\n206:                 pool_options = {}\\n207: \\n208:             try:\\n209:                 from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool\\n210:             except ImportError as err:\\n211:                 raise ImproperlyConfigured(\\n212:                     \\\"Error loading psycopg_pool module.\\\\nDid you install psycopg[pool]?\\\"\\n213:                 ) from err\\n214: \\n215:             connect_kwargs = self.get_connection_params()\\n216:             # Ensure we run in autocommit, Django properly sets it later on.\\n217:             connect_kwargs[\\\"autocommit\\\"] = True\\n218:             enable_checks = self.settings_dict[\\\"CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS\\\"]\\n219:             pool = ConnectionPool(\\n220:                 kwargs=connect_kwargs,\\n221:                 open=False,  # Do not open the pool during startup.\\n222:                 configure=self._configure_connection,\\n223:                 check=ConnectionPool.check_connection if enable_checks else None,\\n224:                 **pool_options,\\n225:             )\\n226:             # setdefault() ensures that multiple threads don't set this in\\n227:             # parallel. Since we do not open the pool during it's init above,\\n228:             # this means that at worst during startup multiple threads generate\\n229:             # pool objects and the first to set it wins.\\n230:             self._connection_pools.setdefault(self.alias, pool)\\n231: \\n232:         return self._connection_pools[self.alias]\\n233: \\n234:     def close_pool(self):\\n235:         if self.pool:\\n236:             self.pool.close()\\n237:             del self._connection_pools[self.alias]\\n238: \\n239:     def get_database_version(self):\\n240:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n241:         Return a tuple of the database's version.\\n242:         E.g. for pg_version 120004, return (12, 4).\\n243:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n244:         return divmod(self.pg_version, 10000)\\n245: \\n246:     def get_connection_params(self):\\n247:         settings_dict = self.settings_dict\\n248:         # None may be used to connect to the default 'postgres' db\\n249:         if settings_dict[\\\"NAME\\\"] == \\\"\\\" and not settings_dict[\\\"OPTIONS\\\"].get(\\\"service\\\"):\\n250:             raise ImproperlyConfigured(\\n251: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/db/backends/postgresql/base.py%3FL180-250\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "180: \n181:     Database = Database\n182:     SchemaEditorClass = DatabaseSchemaEditor\n183:     # Classes instantiated in __init__().\n184:     client_class = DatabaseClient\n185:     creation_class = DatabaseCreation\n186:     features_class = DatabaseFeatures\n187:     introspection_class = DatabaseIntrospection\n188:     ops_class = DatabaseOperations\n189:     # PostgreSQL backend-specific attributes.\n190:     _named_cursor_idx = 0\n191:     _connection_pools = {}\n192: \n193:     @property\n194:     def pool(self):\n195:         pool_options = self.settings_dict[\"OPTIONS\"].get(\"pool\")\n196:         if self.alias == NO_DB_ALIAS or not pool_options:\n197:             return None\n198: \n199:         if self.alias not in self._connection_pools:\n200:             if self.settings_dict.get(\"CONN_MAX_AGE\", 0) != 0:\n201:                 raise ImproperlyConfigured(\n202:                     \"Pooling doesn't support persistent connections.\"\n203:                 )\n204:             #
